front: Philosopher: analyzed the sentence 'the present king of France is bald' to show how phrases can mean nothing by themselves yet still contribute to a statement's meaning, contra Meinong's theory of non-existent objects
work: On Denoting & Theory of Definite Descriptions

front: Philosopher: P.F. Strawson criticized this thinker's theory of definite descriptions in 'On Referring,' arguing that conventional associations of words are linguistic items, not from their use
work: On Denoting & Theory of Definite Descriptions

front: Philosopher: discovered a paradox concerning the set of all sets that do not contain themselves, which exposed an inconsistency in Frege's Basic Law V
Philosopher: developed a 'ramified' type theory to resolve the paradox he derived from Frege, later superseded by Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory
Philosopher: illustrated the burden-of-proof principle by imagining an object too small to be seen by telescopes orbiting between Earth and Mars
work: Russell's Teapot & Why I Am Not a Christian

front: Philosopher: used a postal worker analogy (sorting letters alphabetically vs. geographically) to explain neutral monism — that mental and physical are two ways of organizing the same substance
Philosopher: called compound propositions 'molecular' (composed of multiple 'atomic' propositions) in his philosophy of logical atomism
Philosopher: organized a 1955 manifesto with Einstein condemning nuclear weapons, and later led a tribunal with Sartre that found the US guilty of war crimes in Vietnam
Philosopher: advocated a four-hour workday in 'In Praise of Idleness' and was dismissed from Trinity College for opposing American entry into World War I
Work: criticizes Aquinas for lacking the 'true philosophic spirit' because he finds arguments for conclusions already drawn, in a survey divided into 'Ancient,' 'Catholic,' and 'Modern' philosophy
back: A History of Western Philosophy (Bertrand Russell)

front: Work: argues against coherence theory by noting that coherentists presuppose the laws of logic, and introduces the distinction between appearance and reality using a table example
work: The Problems of Philosophy & Other Works
